segments.value: To scale a vector, multiply each component by c.
A positive c keeps the direction and stretches or shrinks the length. A negative c also flips the vector to point the opposite way.

questions.prompt: Drag the head of the vector to create 3A, then enter the resulting vector.
questions.hint: 3A multiplies each component of A by 3. Drag the head outward until c = 3 and read off ⟨3·2, 3·1⟩.
questions.explanation: 3 · ⟨2, 1⟩ = ⟨3·2, 3·1⟩ = ⟨6, 3⟩. Multiplying by a positive scalar stretches the length while the direction stays the same.

questions.prompt: Drag the head of the vector to create −2A, then enter the resulting vector.
questions.hint: −2A multiplies each component by −2, which flips the direction and doubles the length. Drag the head through the origin until c = −2.
questions.explanation: −2 · ⟨2, 1⟩ = ⟨−2·2, −2·1⟩ = ⟨−4, −2⟩. A negative scalar reverses the direction, and |−2| = 2 doubles the length.

questions.prompt: Drag the head of the vector until c·A matches ⟨6, 3⟩, then enter the scalar c.
questions.hint: Each component of ⟨6, 3⟩ is the same multiple of ⟨2, 1⟩. What number times 2 gives 6?

questions.prompt: Drag the head of the vector until c·A matches ⟨−4, −2⟩, then enter the scalar c.
questions.hint: ⟨−4, −2⟩ points opposite to A and is twice as long, so c must be negative.

questions.prompt: Which of the following could be written as cA? Select all that apply.
questions.hint: A vector is cA only if BOTH components are the same multiple of A. Since A = ⟨3, 2⟩, cA = ⟨3c, 2c⟩.
questions.explanation: ⟨3c, 2c⟩ gives ⟨6, 4⟩ (c = 2), ⟨−3, −2⟩ (c = −1), and ⟨9, 6⟩ (c = 3). The others change the ratio between the components, so they can't be written as cA.
